lessons.title: Concept of Temperature and Thermometric Properties

content.body: When a doctor takes your temperature during a clinic visit in Kampala, or when a farmer checks the temperature of a grain store to prevent spoilage, they rely on thermometry — the science of measuring temperature. But what exactly is temperature? How do we define it precisely, and what properties of matter allow us to measure it? This lesson establishes the foundations of thermometry.
What is Temperature?

**Temperature** is a measure of the degree of hotness or coldness of a body. More precisely, it is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the molecules of a substance.

- Hot body: molecules have high average kinetic energy → high temperature
- Cold body: molecules have low average kinetic energy → low temperature

**Thermal equilibrium:** When two bodies are in thermal contact and no heat flows between them, they are said to be in thermal equilibrium. They have the same temperature.

**Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics:** If body A is in thermal equilibrium with body C, and body B is also in thermal equilibrium with body C, then A and B are in thermal equilibrium with each other.

This law is the basis of thermometry — a thermometer (body C) reaches thermal equilibrium with the object being measured (A or B), and we read its temperature.

**Temperature vs Heat:**
- **Temperature** is a property of a body (intensive — does not depend on the amount of matter). Unit: °C or K.
- **Heat** is energy in transit (flows from high temperature to low temperature). Unit: joule (J).

A large bowl of warm water and a small cup of hot tea may have the same temperature, but the bowl contains more heat energy.
Thermometric Properties

A **thermometric property** is a physical property of a substance that changes measurably, consistently, and reproducibly with temperature. It must:
1. Change continuously and monotonically with temperature
2. Be easily and accurately measurable
3. Give the same reading as other thermometers at fixed points

**Examples of thermometric properties and the thermometers based on them:**

| Thermometric Property | Thermometer Type |
|---|---|
| Length of a liquid column | Liquid-in-glass thermometer (mercury, alcohol) |
| Volume of a gas at constant pressure | Constant-pressure gas thermometer |
| Pressure of a gas at constant volume | Constant-volume gas thermometer |
| Electrical resistance of a metal | Resistance thermometer (Platinum Resistance Thermometer) |
| EMF (voltage) of a thermocouple | Thermoelectric thermometer |
| Intensity of emitted radiation | Optical pyrometer / Infrared thermometer |
| Colour of heated metal | Colour pyrometer |

**Properties of a good thermometric substance:**
- Responds quickly to temperature changes (good thermal conductivity or small mass)
- Gives consistent, reproducible readings
- Has a wide working range
- Easy to use and read
- Safe (non-toxic, non-reactive)

**Why mercury is commonly used in liquid thermometers:**
- Does not wet glass (clear meniscus)
- Expands uniformly with temperature
- Wide liquid range (−39°C to 357°C)
- Easy to see (silvery colour)
- Good thermal conductor (quick response)

**Limitations of mercury thermometers:**
- Poisonous (banned in many countries)
- Cannot measure below −39°C (freezing point)
- Fragile (glass breaks)

**Why alcohol is used as an alternative:**
- Low freezing point (−115°C) → suitable for very cold temperatures
- Non-toxic
- Cheaper than mercury
- Limitation: boiling point only 78°C (limited upper range)
Fixed Points and Temperature Scales

To calibrate a thermometer, we need two **fixed points** — reproducible, universally agreed reference temperatures:

**Lower fixed point (ice point):** The temperature of pure melting ice at standard atmospheric pressure. Defined as 0°C or 273.15 K.

**Upper fixed point (steam point):** The temperature of steam above pure boiling water at standard atmospheric pressure. Defined as 100°C or 373.15 K.

**Why these points?** They are reproducible anywhere in the world with pure water at standard pressure — no special equipment needed.

**Calibrating a liquid-in-glass thermometer:**
1. Place thermometer in pure melting ice → mark the level of the liquid → this is the 0°C mark
2. Place thermometer in steam above boiling water → mark the new level → this is the 100°C mark
3. Divide the interval into 100 equal divisions → each division = 1°C

**Fundamental interval:** The length of the liquid column between the two fixed points.

**Temperature of an unknown point X:**
If the thermometric property has value X at the unknown temperature, value X₀ at the lower fixed point, and value X₁₀₀ at the upper fixed point:

$$\theta = \frac{X - X_0}{X_{100} - X_0} \times 100 \text{ °C}$$

**General formula for empirical temperature:**
$$\theta = \frac{X_\theta - X_0}{X_{100} - X_0} \times 100$$

**Worked Example 1:** A resistance thermometer has resistance 25.0 Ω at 0°C and 35.0 Ω at 100°C. At an unknown temperature, it reads 32.0 Ω. Find the temperature.

θ = (32.0 − 25.0)/(35.0 − 25.0) × 100 = 7.0/10.0 × 100 = 70°C

**Worked Example 2:** A thermocouple gives an EMF of 0 mV at 0°C and 6.0 mV at 100°C. At the temperature of boiling cooking oil in Kampala, it gives 3.6 mV. Find the oil temperature.

θ = (3.6 − 0)/(6.0 − 0) × 100 = 60°C

content.title: Summary
content.body: Temperature measures average molecular KE. Zeroth Law: basis of thermometry. Thermometric property: any physical property that varies reproducibly with temperature. Fixed points: ice point (0°C) and steam point (100°C). Empirical temperature: θ = (Xθ−X₀)/(X₁₀₀−X₀) × 100.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Temperature: Measure of the degree of hotness; related to average molecular kinetic energy; unit: °C or K
• Thermal equilibrium: State in which two bodies in contact have no net heat flow between them; they are at the same temperature
• Thermometric property: A physical property that changes measurably and reproducibly with temperature
• Fixed points: Reproducible reference temperatures used to calibrate thermometers: 0°C (ice point) and 100°C (steam point)
• Empirical temperature: Temperature defined by a thermometric property: θ = (Xθ−X₀)/(X₁₀₀−X₀) × 100

questions.question: Convert 25°C to Kelvin.
What is the temperature of the ice point on the Kelvin scale?
questions.explanation: The ice point is 0°C = 0 + 273 = 273 K.

questions.question: Which thermometric property is used in a platinum resistance thermometer?
options: EMF at a junction of two metals
Pressure of a fixed volume of gas
questions.answer: Electrical resistance of platinum wire
questions.explanation: A platinum resistance thermometer (PRT) uses the fact that the electrical resistance of platinum increases measurably and reproducibly with temperature.

questions.question: Absolute zero is defined as:
options: The lowest possible temperature, equal to −273°C
The temperature at which water vapour condenses
questions.answer: The lowest possible temperature, equal to −273°C
questions.explanation: Absolute zero (0 K) is the lowest possible temperature, at which molecular motion effectively ceases. It equals −273.15°C ≈ −273°C.

questions.question: Which thermometer is most suitable for measuring the temperature of boiling steel (~1500°C)?
questions.answer: Platinum-rhodium thermocouple
questions.explanation: Mercury boils at 357°C — far too low. Alcohol boils at 78°C. PRT is rated to about 850°C. A platinum-rhodium thermocouple operates up to ~1600°C, making it the only suitable choice for 1500°C.

questions.question: A resistance thermometer has R₀ = 20.0 Ω at 0°C and R₁₀₀ = 27.2 Ω at 100°C. At a room temperature, R = 22.5 Ω. What is the room temperature?
A temperature change of 50°C is equivalent to a change of how many Kelvin?
questions.explanation: A temperature *change* is the same in Celsius and Kelvin: ΔT(K) = Δθ(°C) = 50 K. Only the zero point differs between the scales; the size of one degree is the same.

lessons.title: Temperature Scales: Celsius, Kelvin and their Interconversion

content.body: A nurse uses Celsius, a physics laboratory uses Kelvin, and an American recipe book uses Fahrenheit. Different temperature scales exist for historical and practical reasons, but they are all measuring the same physical quantity. In this lesson, we derive and interconvert these scales, with particular attention to the absolute (Kelvin) scale which is fundamental to thermodynamics.
The Celsius Scale

The **Celsius scale** (symbol: °C) is the most common everyday scale, used throughout Uganda and most of the world.

**Definition:**
- Lower fixed point: 0°C (pure melting ice at 1 atm)
- Upper fixed point: 100°C (steam above boiling water at 1 atm)
- 100 equal divisions between the fixed points

**Named after** Anders Celsius (Swedish astronomer, 1742).

The Celsius scale is an **empirical scale** — it is defined by the properties of water at two specific conditions.

**Normal body temperature:** ~37°C
**Kampala's average temperature:** ~23°C
**Bujagali dam water temperature:** ~27°C
The Kelvin (Absolute) Scale

The **Kelvin scale** (symbol: K) is the SI unit of temperature and is used in all scientific calculations. It is also called the **thermodynamic temperature scale** or **absolute temperature scale**.

**Key feature:** The Kelvin scale starts at **absolute zero** — the lowest possible temperature, at which all molecular motion ceases and the internal energy of an ideal gas is zero.

**Absolute zero = 0 K = −273.15°C ≈ −273°C**

**Relationship between Celsius and Kelvin:**

$$T(K) = \theta(°C) + 273.15 \approx \theta(°C) + 273$$

$$\theta(°C) = T(K) - 273$$

**Note:** A temperature *difference* (or change) is the same in both scales:
ΔT(K) = Δθ(°C)

**The Kelvin scale has no negative temperatures** — 0 K is the lowest possible temperature. Absolute zero has never been reached experimentally, though temperatures below 1 nanokelvin have been achieved in laboratories.

**Why use Kelvin in physics?**
Many physical laws involve absolute temperature:
- Ideal Gas Law: PV = nRT (T must be in Kelvin)
- Stefan-Boltzmann Law: P = σT⁴ (T in Kelvin)
- Thermodynamic efficiency: η = 1 − T_cold/T_hot

Using Celsius in these equations gives wrong answers!

**Worked Example 1:** Convert the following:
(a) 37°C to Kelvin → T = 37 + 273 = 310 K
(b) 0°C to Kelvin → T = 0 + 273 = 273 K
(c) −40°C to Kelvin → T = −40 + 273 = 233 K
(d) 500 K to Celsius → θ = 500 − 273 = 227°C
(e) 0 K to Celsius → θ = 0 − 273 = −273°C (absolute zero)

**Special values to remember:**
| Description | °C | K |
|---|---|---|
| Absolute zero | −273 | 0 |
| Ice point | 0 | 273 |
| Steam point | 100 | 373 |
| Normal body | 37 | 310 |
| Sun surface | ~5527 | ~5800 |
The Fahrenheit Scale and Triple Point

**The Fahrenheit scale** (symbol: °F) is used mainly in the USA and in older medical/meteorological contexts.

**Definition:**
- Lower fixed point: 32°F (ice point)
- Upper fixed point: 212°F (steam point)
- 180 divisions between fixed points

**Conversion formulas:**
$$°F = \frac{9}{5} \times °C + 32$$
$$°C = \frac{5}{9} \times (°F - 32)$$

**Worked Example 2:** Convert:
(a) 37°C to °F: F = (9/5 × 37) + 32 = 66.6 + 32 = 98.6°F (normal body temperature)
(b) 100°F to °C: C = 5/9 × (100 − 32) = 5/9 × 68 = 37.8°C
(c) −40°C to °F: F = (9/5 × (−40)) + 32 = −72 + 32 = −40°F
(Note: −40° is where the Celsius and Fahrenheit scales coincide!)

**The Triple Point of Water:**
The **triple point** of water is the unique temperature and pressure at which water can exist simultaneously as solid, liquid, and vapour in equilibrium.
- Temperature: 273.16 K = 0.01°C
- Pressure: 611.73 Pa (much lower than atmospheric pressure)

The triple point is now the primary reference point for the Kelvin scale (more precise than the ice point or steam point because it is a single unique state, not dependent on pressure calibration).

**Modern definition (since 2019 SI redefinition):**
The kelvin is defined by fixing the Boltzmann constant k_B = 1.380649 × 10⁻²³ J/K exactly. This means temperature is fundamentally linked to energy at the molecular level.

**Comparison of temperature scales:**

| Fixed Point | Celsius | Kelvin | Fahrenheit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Absolute zero | −273.15 | 0 | −459.67 |
| Ice point | 0 | 273.15 | 32 |
| Triple point of water | 0.01 | 273.16 | 32.018 |
| Normal body temp | 37 | 310 | 98.6 |
| Steam point | 100 | 373.15 | 212 |

**Worked Example 3:** A patient's temperature is recorded as 103.1°F. Convert to °C and K. Is this dangerously high?

°C = 5/9 × (103.1 − 32) = 5/9 × 71.1 = 39.5°C
K = 39.5 + 273 = 312.5 K

Normal body temperature = 37°C. At 39.5°C, this is a significant fever requiring medical attention — but not immediately life-threatening (dangerous fever ≥ 41°C).

content.title: Summary
content.body: Celsius: 0°C (ice) to 100°C (steam). Kelvin: T(K) = θ(°C) + 273; starts at absolute zero (0K = −273°C). Temperature differences same in both scales. Fahrenheit: °F = (9/5)°C + 32. Triple point of water = 273.16 K. Always use Kelvin in thermodynamic equations.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Absolute zero: The lowest possible temperature (0 K = −273°C) at which molecular motion effectively ceases
• Kelvin scale: Absolute temperature scale starting at 0 K; T(K) = θ(°C) + 273; SI unit of temperature
• Triple point of water: The unique temperature (273.16 K) and pressure at which water exists as solid, liquid, and vapour simultaneously
• Fahrenheit scale: Temperature scale where ice point = 32°F and steam point = 212°F; °F = (9/5)°C + 32

questions.question: A thermocouple thermometer is preferred over a liquid-in-glass thermometer for measuring rapidly changing temperatures because:
options: It has very low thermal mass and responds almost instantaneously
It uses mercury which expands faster
questions.answer: It has very low thermal mass and responds almost instantaneously
questions.explanation: A thermocouple junction is tiny (microscopic weld) — it has negligible thermal mass and reaches thermal equilibrium almost immediately. Liquid-in-glass thermometers have a large bulb that takes 30–60 seconds to equilibrate.

questions.question: Convert 98.6°F to Celsius.
questions.explanation: °C = 5/9 × (°F − 32) = 5/9 × (98.6 − 32) = 5/9 × 66.6 = 37°C. This is normal human body temperature.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of an infrared thermometer over a clinical mercury thermometer for use in a hospital?
options: It is non-contact, making it more hygienic
It uses the gas thermometer scale
questions.answer: It is non-contact, making it more hygienic
questions.explanation: Infrared thermometers measure temperature without contact, eliminating the risk of cross-infection between patients. This was especially important during COVID-19 screening in Uganda.

questions.question: A CVGT has pressure 1.50 × 10⁵ Pa at 0°C. At an unknown temperature the pressure is 1.95 × 10⁵ Pa. What is the temperature in °C?
questions.explanation: T = (P/P₀) × T₀ = (1.95/1.50) × 273 = 1.3 × 273 = 354.9 K ≈ 355 K. θ = 355 − 273 = 82°C. Wait: 355 − 273 = 82°C. Answer is 82°C. Let me recalculate: T = 1.3 × 273 = 355 K → θ = 82°C. Answer A/C (both say 82°C — selecting A).

questions.question: A mercury thermometer and a resistance thermometer are both correctly calibrated at 0°C and 100°C. At a temperature between these fixed points, they give slightly different readings. The most likely reason is:
options: One of them was incorrectly calibrated
Mercury expands and electrical resistance change at different rates with temperature
The thermometers were placed in different locations
The Kelvin scale is not accurate between fixed points
questions.answer: Mercury expands and electrical resistance change at different rates with temperature
questions.explanation: Different thermometric properties (liquid expansion, electrical resistance) do not vary in exactly the same way with thermodynamic temperature. Both thermometers agree at the fixed points but diverge between them — each gives its own 'empirical' temperature.

questions.question: The triple point of water is:
questions.answer: 0.01°C and 611 Pa
questions.explanation: The triple point of water (where solid, liquid, and vapour coexist) is at 0.01°C (273.16 K) and 611.73 Pa — well below atmospheric pressure. It is used as the primary fixed point for the Kelvin scale.

questions.question: Convert 150°C to Kelvin, 77K to °C, and 212°F to °C.

content.body: The most familiar thermometer is the liquid-in-glass type — simple, robust, and inexpensive. But for precise scientific measurements, or for temperatures beyond the range of liquids, gas thermometers provide a more fundamental and accurate standard. In this lesson we study both types in detail, including their construction, operation, advantages, and limitations.
Liquid-in-Glass Thermometer

**Construction:**
- A thin-walled glass bulb (large thermal capacity to absorb heat quickly)
- A narrow capillary tube (small bore magnifies the expansion)
- A liquid filling (mercury or alcohol)
- A graduated scale

**Principle of operation:**
The liquid expands more than the glass when temperature rises. The excess liquid rises up the narrow capillary tube. The height of the liquid column is proportional to temperature (within the calibrated range).

**Sensitivity:** Depends on:
1. Bore size of capillary — narrower bore → higher sensitivity (greater rise per degree)
2. Size of bulb — larger bulb → more liquid → greater volume change → higher sensitivity
3. Expansivity of liquid — mercury and alcohol have different thermal expansivities

**Range:**
- Mercury thermometer: −39°C to 357°C
- Alcohol thermometer: −115°C to 78°C

**Clinical thermometer (medical use in Uganda):**
- Range: 35°C to 42°C (narrow range for detecting fever)
- Has a constriction just above the bulb — prevents mercury from falling back when removed from patient (allows leisurely reading)
- Sterilised between patients
- Now largely replaced by digital thermometers in Uganda's hospitals

**Laboratory thermometer:**
- Range: −10°C to 110°C (or wider)
- No constriction
- Less sensitive than clinical thermometer (wider bore)

**Advantages of liquid-in-glass:**
- Simple, cheap, portable
- No external power needed
- Direct reading
- Robust for field use

**Disadvantages:**
- Fragile
- Mercury is toxic
- Cannot be used for very high/low temperatures
- Cannot record or transmit data electronically
- Slow response time for rapidly changing temperatures
- Cannot be read remotely
Constant-Volume Gas Thermometer

The **constant-volume gas thermometer** (CVGT) is the most accurate thermometer for scientific measurements and defines the ideal gas temperature scale.

**Construction:**
- A glass bulb containing a fixed amount of dry gas (usually hydrogen or helium)
- Connected to a mercury manometer via a thin capillary tube
- A reference mark on the capillary indicates constant volume
- Mercury reservoir can be raised/lowered to adjust the mercury level back to the reference mark

**Principle of operation:**
At constant volume, the pressure of an ideal gas is proportional to its absolute temperature:
**P ∝ T** (constant V and n)
or P/T = constant

When the temperature changes, the gas pressure changes. Mercury is adjusted to maintain constant volume (reference mark). The pressure is read from the manometer.

**Temperature measurement:**
Knowing P₀ (pressure at ice point = 273.15 K) and Pθ (pressure at unknown temperature):
$$T = \frac{P}{P_0} \times 273.15 \text{ K}$$

or using the two-fixed-point empirical formula:
$$\theta = \frac{P_\theta - P_0}{P_{100} - P_0} \times 100 \text{ °C}$$

**Why the CVGT is fundamental:**
For an ideal gas, the relationship P ∝ T is exact. Real gases (H₂, He) at low pressures behave nearly ideally. The CVGT therefore gives temperatures on a scale that is independent of the specific gas used (in the ideal limit) — this is the absolute thermodynamic scale.

**Worked Example 1:** A CVGT with hydrogen gas has a pressure of 1.00 × 10⁵ Pa at 0°C and 1.366 × 10⁵ Pa at an unknown temperature. Find the temperature.

θ = (P − P₀)/(P₁₀₀ − P₀) × 100
First, find P₁₀₀: At 100°C = 373.15 K, P₁₀₀ = P₀ × 373.15/273.15 = 1.00×10⁵ × 1.366 = 1.366×10⁵ Pa

Actually, at θ: T = (Pθ/P₀) × 273.15 = (1.366×10⁵/1.00×10⁵) × 273.15 = 1.366 × 273.15 = 373.1 K = 100.0°C

**Worked Example 2:** A CVGT shows pressures of 1.500 × 10⁵ Pa at 0°C and 2.050 × 10⁵ Pa at 100°C. At the temperature of a furnace, the pressure is 4.200 × 10⁵ Pa. Find the furnace temperature.

θ = (4.200 − 1.500)/(2.050 − 1.500) × 100 = 2.700/0.550 × 100 = 490.9°C

**Advantages of CVGT:**
- Highly accurate
- Provides the standard for all other thermometers
- Based on fundamental gas law

**Disadvantages:**
- Bulky and fragile
- Slow response
- Inconvenient for routine use
- The gas must be corrected for non-ideal behaviour at higher pressures
Constant-Pressure Gas Thermometer

In the **constant-pressure gas thermometer**, the gas pressure is kept constant (usually at 1 atm), and the volume of the gas is the thermometric property.

**Principle:**
At constant pressure, the volume of an ideal gas is proportional to absolute temperature:
**V ∝ T** (constant P and n) — Charles's Law

**Construction:** A graduated tube (like a syringe) holds the gas. As temperature changes, the piston moves to maintain constant pressure. The volume of gas is read from the graduated scale.

**Temperature:**
$$\theta = \frac{V_\theta - V_0}{V_{100} - V_0} \times 100$$

or: T = (V/V₀) × 273.15 K

**Comparison with CVGT:**
- Both give the same temperature for ideal gases
- CVGT more commonly used in practice (easier to keep V constant than P constant)
- Both define the gas temperature scale

**Agreement between thermometers:**
All properly calibrated thermometers agree at the fixed points (0°C and 100°C). However, between the fixed points, different thermometers may give slightly different readings because different thermometric properties do not change linearly in the same way with temperature. The CVGT (ideal gas) is taken as the standard.

content.title: Summary
content.body: Liquid-in-glass: liquid expansion in capillary tube; mercury (−39°C to 357°C) or alcohol (−115°C to 78°C). CVGT: P∝T at constant V; most accurate; defines absolute scale. Temperature from CVGT: θ = (Pθ−P₀)/(P₁₀₀−P₀)×100. Clinical thermometer has constriction to hold reading.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Constant-volume gas thermometer: Uses gas pressure at constant volume as thermometric property; most accurate; defines absolute temperature scale
• Clinical thermometer: Liquid-in-glass thermometer with a constriction that holds the reading after removal from patient
• Sensitivity (thermometer): The change in thermometric property per degree change in temperature; higher for narrow-bore capillaries and large bulbs
• Range: The span of temperatures over which a thermometer can give accurate readings

questions.question: Which correctly states three properties of a good thermometric substance?
options: Changes measurably with temperature; gives reproducible readings; easy to measure accurately
Must be a liquid; must expand; must be silver
Freezes at 0°C; boils at 100°C; is transparent
questions.answer: Changes measurably with temperature; gives reproducible readings; easy to measure accurately
questions.explanation: A thermometric substance must: (1) change measurably and monotonically with T, (2) give reproducible/consistent results, (3) be easily and accurately measurable. Mercury also: uniform expansion, clear meniscus, wide range.

questions.question: A thermocouple gives 0mV at 0°C and 6.40mV at 100°C. An autoclave reads 8.96mV. What is the temperature?
questions.explanation: θ=(8.96−0)/(6.40−0)×100=8.96/6.40×100=140°C. Typical steam autoclave sterilisation temperature.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of a thermocouple over a mercury thermometer?
options: Thermocouple is cheaper and easier to use
Thermocouple has much wider range (up to 1600°C vs 357°C) and near-instantaneous response
Thermocouple is more accurate at body temperature
questions.answer: Thermocouple has much wider range (up to 1600°C vs 357°C) and near-instantaneous response
questions.explanation: Thermocouples (Pt-Rh type) operate up to ~1600°C vs mercury's 357°C. The tiny junction has negligible thermal mass — responds almost instantly to temperature changes. Ideal for high-T and rapidly changing temperatures.

questions.question: Why do different thermometers give slightly different readings between the fixed points?
options: One was incorrectly calibrated at the fixed points
Different thermometric properties (Hg expansion, resistance) vary non-linearly with true thermodynamic temperature in different ways
The fixed points themselves are different temperatures
The thermometers are at different heights causing pressure differences
questions.answer: Different thermometric properties (Hg expansion, resistance) vary non-linearly with true thermodynamic temperature in different ways
questions.explanation: All thermometers agree at 0°C and 100°C (fixed points). Between them, mercury volume and platinum resistance follow different non-linear curves vs true thermodynamic temperature. The gas thermometer provides the true standard.

questions.question: A CVGT: P₀=1.80×10⁵ Pa at 0°C; P=2.46×10⁵ Pa at unknown T. (a) Find T in °C. (b) What pressure at −50°C?
A platinum wire: R=R₀(1+αθ), α=3.92×10⁻³°C⁻¹, R₀=50Ω. At 200°C, what is R?
What are the two fixed points used to calibrate a thermometer?
options: Body temperature and boiling point of alcohol
Melting point of pure ice and boiling point of pure water at standard pressure
Absolute zero and boiling point of water
Triple point of water and melting point of gold
questions.answer: Melting point of pure ice and boiling point of pure water at standard pressure
questions.explanation: The lower fixed point is the temperature of pure melting ice at 1 atm (0°C). The upper fixed point is the temperature of steam above boiling pure water at 1 atm (100°C). These two are reproducible worldwide.

lessons.title: Electrical Thermometers: Resistance, Thermocouple and Infrared

content.body: Modern thermometry has moved far beyond mercury-in-glass. Hospitals now use infrared ear thermometers. Industrial furnaces are monitored by thermocouples. Precision laboratories use platinum resistance thermometers. These electrical thermometers offer remote reading, digital output, faster response, and wider ranges. In this lesson we study the three main electrical thermometer types.
Platinum Resistance Thermometer (PRT)

**Principle:** The electrical resistance of metals increases with temperature. For platinum, this relationship is well-defined and reproducible:

**R_θ = R₀(1 + αθ + βθ²)**

Where R₀ is resistance at 0°C, and α and β are material constants for platinum.

For practical purposes within moderate ranges, the relationship is approximately linear:
**R_θ ≈ R₀(1 + αθ)**

Where α ≈ 3.85 × 10⁻³ K⁻¹ for platinum.

**Empirical temperature from resistance:**
$$\theta = \frac{R_\theta - R_0}{R_{100} - R_0} \times 100$$

**Why platinum?**
- Chemically inert (does not oxidise or corrode)
- High melting point (1768°C) — very wide range
- Highly reproducible and stable
- Well-characterised resistance-temperature relationship
- Can be drawn into very fine, pure wire

**Range:** −200°C to +850°C (standard), up to 1000°C (special grades)

**Construction:**
- Very fine platinum wire wound on a ceramic or glass former
- Encased in a protective metal sheath
- Connected to a precision Wheatstone bridge circuit for accurate resistance measurement

**Applications:**
- National standards laboratories (defining the ITS-90 temperature scale)
- Hospital sterilisers and incubators
- Food processing (pasteurisation)
- Industrial process control
- Meteorological stations

**Thermistor (semiconductor resistance thermometer):**
A thermistor is made from semiconductor materials (metal oxides). Its resistance **decreases** rapidly with increasing temperature (negative temperature coefficient, NTC).

The thermistor resistance changes much more per degree than platinum, making it more sensitive — but less linear and less stable. Used in digital thermometers, medical devices, and electronic temperature control circuits.

**Worked Example 1:** A platinum resistance thermometer has R₀ = 25.0 Ω and R₁₀₀ = 35.4 Ω. At a certain temperature, R = 30.0 Ω. Find the temperature.

θ = (30.0 − 25.0)/(35.4 − 25.0) × 100 = 5.0/10.4 × 100 = 48.1°C
Thermoelectric Thermometer (Thermocouple)

**Principle (Seebeck Effect):** When two different metals are joined at their ends to form a circuit, and the two junctions are at different temperatures, an electromotive force (EMF) is generated. This is the **Seebeck effect**.

The EMF depends on:
- The temperature difference between the two junctions
- The types of metals used

**EMF ≈ aΔT + bΔT²** (a and b are constants for the metal pair)

**At small temperature differences:** EMF ≈ aΔT (approximately linear)

**Construction:**
- Two wires of different metals (e.g., copper-constantan, iron-constantan, platinum-rhodium) joined at both ends
- One junction is the **measuring (hot) junction** — placed at the temperature to be measured
- The other is the **reference (cold) junction** — kept at a known temperature (usually 0°C in ice)
- A sensitive millivoltmeter or digital meter measures the EMF

**Common thermocouple types:**
| Type | Metals | Range |
|---|---|---|
| K | Chromel-Alumel | −200°C to 1260°C |
| J | Iron-Constantan | −40°C to 750°C |
| T | Copper-Constantan | −200°C to 350°C |
| R/S | Platinum-Rhodium | 0°C to 1600°C |

**Empirical temperature:**
$$\theta = \frac{E_\theta - E_0}{E_{100} - E_0} \times 100$$

**Advantages of thermocouples:**
- Very wide range (especially platinum-rhodium: up to 1600°C)
- Very fast response (tiny junction — no thermal mass)
- Can be made very small (e.g., in a hypodermic needle for tissue temperature)
- Robust — no liquid, no fragile glass
- Can be read remotely and logged electronically

**Disadvantages:**
- Low EMF requires sensitive meter (error-prone)
- Non-linear (needs calibration table or equation)
- Reference junction must be controlled carefully

**Applications in Uganda:**
- Kampala cement factories: monitoring kiln temperatures (~1400°C)
- Steel fabrication workshops: measuring metal temperatures
- Research laboratories
- Industrial boilers and steam systems

**Worked Example 2:** A copper-constantan thermocouple gives 0 mV at 0°C, 4.28 mV at 100°C, and an unknown reading at a room temperature. If the EMF is 2.14 mV, find the temperature.

θ = (2.14 − 0)/(4.28 − 0) × 100 = 50°C
Infrared and Digital Thermometers

**Infrared (IR) Thermometer:**

**Principle:** All objects above absolute zero emit electromagnetic radiation. The intensity and spectrum of this radiation depend on temperature (Stefan-Boltzmann Law: P ∝ T⁴). By measuring the infrared radiation emitted by an object, its temperature can be determined without contact.

**Construction:**
- An optical lens focuses infrared radiation from the target onto an IR detector (thermopile or pyroelectric sensor)
- Electronic circuitry converts the detector signal to temperature
- A digital display shows the temperature
- A laser pointer (in gun-type devices) shows where the measurement is aimed

**Range:** Typically −50°C to 1000°C (depends on model)

**Key parameter — emissivity (ε):** The efficiency with which an object radiates IR compared to a perfect blackbody. ε = 1 for a perfect blackbody; less for shiny metals. The thermometer must be set to the correct emissivity of the target.

**Advantages:**
- Non-contact (hygienic — important in medical use, especially post-COVID-19 in Uganda)
- Instantaneous reading
- Can measure very hot or moving objects safely
- No risk of contamination

**Disadvantages:**
- Emissivity must be known and set correctly
- Affected by dust, steam, or glass between sensor and target
- Less accurate than contact thermometers
- Measures surface temperature only (not deep tissue temperature)

**Applications:**
- Fever screening (used extensively in Ugandan airports, hospitals, and schools during COVID-19)
- Industrial: measuring furnace walls, electrical equipment (hot spots)
- Food industry: checking temperature of food without touching it
- Building inspection: finding heat leaks

**Optical Pyrometer:**
Measures the temperature of very hot bodies (above ~700°C) by comparing the colour (wavelength distribution) of light emitted with that of a standard source. The operator adjusts the filament current of a standard lamp until it appears to vanish against the hot background.

**Range:** ~700°C to above 3000°C
**Applications:** Steel furnaces, glass manufacture, molten metal monitoring

**Note:** Total radiation pyrometer is NOT required per NCDC 2025 syllabus.

**Digital thermometers (electronic):**
Use a thermistor or IC temperature sensor. Common in households, clinics, weather stations. Read-out is digital. Range typically −50°C to 150°C. Now used routinely in Ugandan health centres.

content.title: Summary
content.body: PRT: resistance increases with T; θ = (Rθ−R₀)/(R₁₀₀−R₀)×100; platinum is inert, stable, wide range. Thermocouple: Seebeck effect; EMF depends on ΔT between junctions; very wide range (up to 1600°C); fast response. IR thermometer: measures emitted radiation; non-contact; important for infection control. Optical pyrometer: colour of hot bodies; >700°C.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Platinum resistance thermometer (PRT): Uses increase of platinum resistance with temperature; R = R₀(1 + αθ); highly accurate; range −200°C to 850°C
• Thermocouple (Seebeck effect): Two dissimilar metals joined at both ends; EMF generated when junctions are at different temperatures
• Thermistor: Semiconductor resistance thermometer; resistance decreases with temperature (NTC); high sensitivity
• Infrared thermometer: Measures emitted infrared radiation to determine temperature without contact; important for medical screening

questions.question: Why does the clinical thermometer have a constriction just above the bulb?
options: To prevent mercury from expanding too quickly
To hold the mercury reading after removal from the patient
To prevent the mercury from freezing
questions.answer: To hold the mercury reading after removal from the patient
questions.explanation: When the clinical thermometer is removed from the patient, the mercury begins to cool and contract. The constriction prevents the mercury in the capillary from falling back into the bulb, preserving the reading so the nurse can read it at leisure. It is reset by shaking.

questions.question: To calibrate a liquid-in-glass thermometer using two fixed points, what does the student mark at 0°C?
options: The level when placed in boiling water
The level when placed in pure melting ice at 1 atm
The level at normal room temperature
questions.answer: The level when placed in pure melting ice at 1 atm
questions.explanation: Lower fixed point = pure melting ice at standard atmospheric pressure = 0°C. Upper fixed point = steam above boiling pure water at standard pressure = 100°C. Then divide the interval into 100 equal divisions.

questions.question: The Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics is important to thermometry because:
options: It states that heat always flows from hot to cold
It justifies using a thermometer — if thermometer and object are in thermal equilibrium, they have the same temperature
It proves that temperature is proportional to molecular kinetic energy
questions.answer: It justifies using a thermometer — if thermometer and object are in thermal equilibrium, they have the same temperature
questions.explanation: The Zeroth Law states that if A is in equilibrium with C, and B is in equilibrium with C, then A and B are at the same temperature. This justifies thermometry: the thermometer (C) reaches equilibrium with the object (A or B) and then with a calibrated scale — allowing us to measure the object's temperature.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of a platinum resistance thermometer over a thermocouple?
options: PRT has a wider range than any thermocouple
PRT has higher accuracy and stability; platinum is chemically inert with a very reproducible resistance-temperature relationship
PRT is cheaper and simpler to use
PRT responds more quickly to temperature changes
questions.answer: PRT has higher accuracy and stability; platinum is chemically inert with a very reproducible resistance-temperature relationship
questions.explanation: PRT advantages: very high accuracy (±0.001°C achievable); platinum is chemically inert and stable; highly reproducible R-T relationship. Used as a standards instrument (ITS-90). Main disadvantage: self-heating error.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly states the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed
Heat flows from hot bodies to cold bodies
If A is in thermal equilibrium with C, and B is in thermal equilibrium with C, then A and B are in thermal equilibrium with each other
The entropy of the universe always increases
questions.answer: If A is in thermal equilibrium with C, and B is in thermal equilibrium with C, then A and B are in thermal equilibrium with each other
questions.explanation: The Zeroth Law: if A ↔ C and B ↔ C (thermal equilibrium), then A ↔ B. This is the basis for using thermometers — the thermometer acts as the 'C' common reference.

questions.question: In Searle's bar method for k of a good conductor, what does the formula k = ṁ_water×c_water×ΔT_water×d / (A×ΔT_bar) represent?
options: Heat conducted through bar = heat removed by cooling water per second
Heat radiated from bar = heat convected from water
Fourier's law applied to the water flow
questions.answer: Heat conducted through bar = heat removed by cooling water per second
questions.explanation: At steady state: heat conducted along bar per second = heat absorbed by cooling water per second. kA(T₁−T₂)/d = ṁ×c_water×ΔT_water. Solving: k = ṁ×c_water×ΔT_water×d/(A×ΔT_bar).

lessons.title: Comparison of Thermometers and Problem Solving

content.body: No single thermometer is best for all situations. A doctor taking a patient's temperature, an engineer monitoring a furnace, a meteorologist measuring air temperature, and a food inspector checking refrigerator temperatures all need different instruments. In this lesson we compare thermometers systematically, work through examination-style problems, and develop the ability to select the right thermometer for a given application.
Systematic Comparison of Thermometers

**Summary comparison table:**

| Property | Liquid-in-glass | CVGT | PRT | Thermocouple | IR thermometer |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Thermometric property | Liquid expansion | Gas pressure | Electrical resistance | EMF (voltage) | Infrared radiation |
| Range | −115°C to 357°C | −270°C to 1500°C+ | −200°C to 850°C | −200°C to 1600°C | −50°C to 3000°C |
| Sensitivity | Medium | Very high | High | Medium | Medium |
| Response time | Slow (30–60 s) | Very slow (minutes) | Fast (seconds) | Very fast (<1s) | Instantaneous |
| Accuracy | Moderate | Very high | Very high | Moderate | Moderate |
| Contact needed? | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes (hot junction) | No |
| Remote reading? | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Portability | Good | Poor | Good | Good | Good |
| Cost | Low | High | Medium | Low | Medium |
| Main application | General purpose | Scientific standard | Precision industrial | High-temperature | Medical/Industrial |

**Selecting the right thermometer:**
1. **What is the temperature range?**
   - Below −39°C → alcohol, PRT, or thermocouple
   - Above 357°C → PRT, thermocouple, or optical pyrometer
   - Above 1000°C → thermocouple (platinum type) or optical pyrometer

2. **How quickly does temperature change?**
   - Rapidly changing → thermocouple or IR thermometer
   - Slow changes → any type

3. **Is contact possible?**
   - Moving object, sterile field, food → IR thermometer
   - Fixed object → any contact type

4. **What accuracy is needed?**
   - Scientific standard → CVGT
   - Precise industrial → PRT
   - Routine measurement → liquid-in-glass or digital

5. **Remote/electronic logging needed?**
   - Yes → PRT or thermocouple (electronic output)
   - No → liquid-in-glass or CVGT
Disagreement Between Thermometers

Different thermometers give the same reading only at the fixed points (0°C and 100°C). Between these points, they may disagree because the thermometric properties do not all change linearly with temperature in the same way.

**Example:** A mercury thermometer and an alcohol thermometer are both calibrated at 0°C and 100°C. At 50°C on the mercury thermometer, the alcohol thermometer might read 50.2°C because mercury and alcohol expand at different rates between the fixed points.

**The Gas Scale as Standard:**
The constant-volume gas thermometer (with ideal gas) provides the true thermodynamic temperature scale. All other thermometers are calibrated against it. The International Temperature Scale (ITS-90) defines a set of fixed points beyond 0°C and 100°C that allow thermometers to be calibrated over their full range.

**ITS-90 Fixed Points (selected):**
| Reference Point | Temperature |
|---|---|
| Triple point of hydrogen | 13.8033 K |
| Triple point of water | 273.16 K |
| Freezing point of tin | 505.08 K = 231.93°C |
| Freezing point of silver | 1234.93 K = 961.78°C |
| Freezing point of gold | 1337.33 K = 1064.18°C |

**Worked Example 1:** A mercury thermometer reads 50.0°C and a resistance thermometer reads 49.5°C at the same temperature. Which is correct?

Neither is necessarily 'correct' in absolute terms — both are empirical scales. The resistance thermometer (PRT) is likely closer to the true thermodynamic temperature because platinum resistance varies more uniformly with thermodynamic temperature than mercury volume does. To resolve, compare with a CVGT at the same temperature.
Worked Problems: Temperature Scales and Thermometers

**Problem 1 (Celsius/Kelvin):** The temperature in Kabale on a cold night is −3°C. Convert to Kelvin and Fahrenheit.
T(K) = −3 + 273 = 270 K
T(°F) = (9/5 × (−3)) + 32 = −5.4 + 32 = 26.6°F

**Problem 2 (Empirical temperature — thermocouple):** A thermocouple gives EMF values of 0 mV at 0°C, 5.27 mV at 100°C, and 3.16 mV at a temperature θ. Find θ.

θ = (3.16 − 0)/(5.27 − 0) × 100 = 59.96°C ≈ 60°C

**Problem 3 (Resistance thermometer):** A platinum wire has resistance 100 Ω at 0°C and 138.5 Ω at 100°C. At the temperature of a patient, it reads 114.6 Ω. Find the patient's temperature.

θ = (114.6 − 100)/(138.5 − 100) × 100 = 14.6/38.5 × 100 = 37.9°C
(Slight fever — normal is 37°C)

**Problem 4 (CVGT):** A constant-volume gas thermometer has a pressure of 1.20 × 10⁵ Pa at 0°C. At the boiling point of sulphur, the pressure is 2.34 × 10⁵ Pa. Find the boiling point of sulphur in °C.

Using T = (P/P₀) × 273.15:
T = (2.34/1.20) × 273.15 = 1.95 × 273.15 = 532.6 K = 532.6 − 273.15 = 259.4°C

**Problem 5 (Thermometer selection):** State which thermometer is most suitable for each of the following and give a reason:

(a) Measuring the temperature of molten iron in a steel factory (~1500°C)
→ Thermocouple (platinum-rhodium type) or optical pyrometer — only these have sufficient range.

(b) Measuring a patient's body temperature in a busy Ugandan hospital
→ Digital thermometer (thermistor-based) or IR thermometer — fast, hygienic, no risk of cross-infection.

(c) Measuring the temperature of liquid nitrogen (−196°C)
→ Platinum resistance thermometer (range down to −200°C) or thermocouple.

(d) Monitoring a controlled furnace in a ceramics factory that must be kept at 450°C ± 2°C
→ Platinum resistance thermometer — high accuracy, stable, can give continuous electronic output for automatic control.

(e) Measuring the temperature of the exhaust gas from a boda-boda engine
→ Thermocouple — fast response, can withstand vibration, wide range.

content.title: Summary
content.body: No single thermometer suits all applications. Choose based on range, response time, contact requirement, accuracy, and electronic output needs. Different thermometers disagree between fixed points — CVGT gives the true thermodynamic temperature. Key empirical formula: θ = (Xθ−X₀)/(X₁₀₀−X₀) × 100 applies to all types.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• ITS-90: International Temperature Scale of 1990 — defines multiple fixed points for calibrating thermometers over their full range
• Emissivity: The ratio of radiation emitted by a surface to that of a perfect blackbody; must be set correctly for IR thermometers
• Seebeck effect: Generation of an EMF when two dissimilar metal junctions are at different temperatures — basis of thermocouple operation

questions.question: At what temperature are the Celsius and Fahrenheit scales equal?
Compare CVGT and PRT: which has faster response time and which is more accurate?
options: PRT faster (seconds vs minutes for CVGT); CVGT most accurate (defines absolute scale)
Both respond equally fast; CVGT more accurate
questions.answer: PRT faster (seconds vs minutes for CVGT); CVGT most accurate (defines absolute scale)
questions.explanation: CVGT: very accurate (primary thermodynamic standard) but very slow (large thermal mass, minutes to equilibrate). PRT: responds in seconds; highly accurate but calibrated against the CVGT standard.

questions.question: A thermometer is used to measure the temperature of boiling ethanol. At the ice point the thermometric property X = 10 units; at the steam point X = 50 units; at the boiling point of ethanol X = 38 units. What temperature does this thermometer record for boiling ethanol?
Why is alcohol preferred over mercury for thermometers in cold highland areas like Kabale?
options: Alcohol freezes at −115°C vs mercury at −39°C — alcohol remains liquid at temperatures that would freeze mercury solid
Alcohol expands more uniformly than mercury
Alcohol is non-toxic and safer for schoolchildren
questions.answer: Alcohol freezes at −115°C vs mercury at −39°C — alcohol remains liquid at temperatures that would freeze mercury solid
questions.explanation: The key thermometric advantage: alcohol stays liquid down to −115°C, well below mercury's freezing point of −39°C. This makes alcohol essential for low-temperature thermometry, even if mercury has other advantages.

questions.question: In a constant-volume gas thermometer, the volume is kept constant by:
options: Cooling the gas when temperature rises
Adjusting a mercury column so the gas meniscus returns to a reference mark
Using a rigid sealed metal container
Connecting the gas to the atmosphere
questions.answer: Adjusting a mercury column so the gas meniscus returns to a reference mark
questions.explanation: When the gas temperature changes, the gas would expand or contract. In the CVGT, the mercury reservoir is raised or lowered until the gas-mercury meniscus returns to a fixed reference mark (fixed volume). The new mercury height then gives the gas pressure via the manometer.

questions.question: A student makes a thermometer from a glass tube with coloured water. To increase its sensitivity they should:
options: Use a narrower bore capillary tube and/or a larger bulb
Use a longer tube at the same bore
questions.answer: Use a narrower bore capillary tube and/or a larger bulb
questions.explanation: Sensitivity = change in thermometric property per degree. Narrower bore → same volume change produces greater column height change. Larger bulb → more liquid → greater volume change per degree. Both increase sensitivity.
